mappings.rationaleAI: The Mongol Empire's founding by Genghis Khan, expansion under successors, military organization, administrative reforms, and fracture into four successor khanates (Yuan, Ilkhanate, Golden Horde, Chagatai) directly address the establishment of the Mongol Empire (C.1) and its successor states (C.2).

Timur's establishment of the Timurid dynasty, his capital at Samarkand, conquests across Afghanistan, Iran, and Central Asia, and the succession under his descendants directly address the Timurid section (C.2.c).
Genghis Khan's unification of Mongol tribes, military and political organization, tactics, and conquests (C.1.a), and the division of his empire among his sons with further expansion (C.1.b).

The Silk Road's overland routes through Central Asia, transmission of religions, and Sogdian facilitation relate to interactions between steppe peoples and neighbouring civilizations (A), and the Hsiung-nu confederation's relationship to Han China's trade routes (B.1).
The Tang dynasty's tributary relations with Central Asian states and Silk Road trade provide context for the Hsiung-nu and other Inner Asian peoples' interactions with Chinese empires (B.1).
The Han dynasty's military campaigns against the Xiongnu and expansion into Central Asia directly address the Hsiung-nu tribal confederation and Han China's pressure on it (B.1).
Alexander's conquests reached into Central Asia and Afghanistan, relevant to the development of Afghanistan under Achaemenian and Greek rule (B.5).
The Islamic Golden Age's intellectual production and its connection to Central Asian urban centers relates to Muslim rule and cultural developments in West Turkistan (B.3.a).
The Achaemenid Empire's extension to the Indus Valley and Central Asian territories relates to Afghanistan's rule by Achaemenians (B.5).
The post-classical period's Mongol Empire coverage and Silk Road trade networks provide broad context for peoples and states of Inner Asia (B) and the Mongol Empire and its successor states (C).
The Mughal Empire's Timurid-Mongol origins and Babur as Timur's descendant connect to the Timurid dynasty and its successors in Turkistan, Afghanistan, and Transoxania (C.2.c).
Iran's Safavid dynasty and its interactions with Central Asian powers relate to the Iranian Il-Khans and successor states (C.2.d).
Russia's expansion from Kievan Rus' through Tsardom relates to Russian expansion into Siberia and western Turkistan as part of the waning of nomad power (E).
The Himalayas' geographic and cultural significance to Tibetan civilizations and Buddhism relates to the section on Tibet and Nepal (D).
Mahayana Buddhism including Tibetan Buddhism (Vajrayana subset) relates to the introduction and resurgence of Buddhism in Tibet (D.1.a, D.1.b) and the revival of Buddhism in Mongolia (C.2.e).

Ibn Khaldun's theories on nomadic vs. sedentary civilizations and cyclical dynastic rise-and-fall provide analytical context for the peoples of the steppes and their interactions with neighbouring civilizations (A).
Russian expansion into Siberia during the Age of Discovery relates to the waning of nomad power and Manchu/Russian expansion into Inner Asia (E).

China's dynastic history intersects with Inner Asian peoples: Han campaigns against Xiongnu (B.1), Manchurian tribes' relations with Chinese (B.2), Yuan dynasty conquest (C.2.a), and Mongolia under Chinese overlordship (C.2.e).
Medieval period coverage of the Mongol Empire and its impact on Eurasia provides context for Inner Asian peoples and states (B) and the Mongol Empire's successor states (C).
